Title: Keeping Your Portfolio Safe In An Unsafe Market 
 Content: ['The U.S. economy is riding on margin, just rolling along with asset prices (stocks, real estate, bonds, etc.) rising on a wave of debt. We may have higher to go, but the end could get messy.', 'The markets this past quarter have been on a bit of a roller coaster going up sharply in April and then dropping like a rock in May, only to recover in June. The U.S. economy could run into some problems in the not-too-distant future. What is an investor to do? Letâ\x80\x99s look at some of the problems first.', "The Federal Reserve's Balance sheet had grown from $1 trillion in 2008 to its current $8 trillion in 2021. If you do the math and annualize that rate, it comes out to a growth rate of 17.34%. Now the goal of the Federal Reserve has always been to keep its balance sheet under $1.5 Trillion except for emergencies like World War II and the Pandemic but going to $8 Trillion means they will have to taper by $6.5 Trillion at some point going forward unless it intends to monetize the debt. And if the Fed admits that it is monetizing debt it risks losing credibility. That could spell disaster for the US dollar. If you believe the Central Bank of the U.S. will ever be able to reduce its balance sheet by that much, then I have a bridge in Brooklyn that I can offer to sell to you real cheap. Why do I say that? Well, government spending has completely gone off the rails, that's why.", 'The Clock is Ticking on U.S. Debt. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'The national debt is at $28.4 Trillion right now but here is what it looks like when you chart it out and compare to where it was as far back as 1900:', '', 'Source: futuretimeline/Pinterest', 'As you can see 2020 was just $23.2 Trillion but recently we hit $28.4 Trillion for an annualized growth rate of 22.41% in just one year and in just one year we added $5.2 Trillion in debt. That is equal to the debt accumulated during all of the first 92 years on the chart above. And that could just be a start to what may be coming.', 'Up until 2000, Main Street has been able to keep up with the debt as our GDP (Gross Domestic Product) has come in around $22 Trillion, so you had about a 100% or so Debt to GDP ratio. But 2021 has changed all that and now we are at 128%. ', 'The Generation that survived World War II is considered the greatest generation and the comforts we have today are due to their defeating Hitler. The current Generation could unfortunately go down in history as the worst generation because of what is projected to happen by 2050 when we pass the baton to your grandchildren. Source: Brookings Institute', 'In 2020 our Debt to GDP was 100% and by 2050 the Congressional Budget Office projects it to be 200%, or twice what it was when we fought World War II. After the War, the greatest generation brought it down from 102% to 35% in 1980, but our generation can never do so, simply because the interest on the national debt will eventually be the largest annual federal expenditure someday. Currently the annual tax revenue comes in at: ', 'And here is where most of that money goes: ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now just those 4 budget items are equal to more than the revenue coming in, so anything else in the budget needs to be paid for by debt. Get ready for tax hikes folks!!! And even that will not be enough.', 'The interest on the debt is $400 billion annually (about $1,200 per person in the US) now but that is only because interest rates are so low. The Federal Reserve cannot raise interest rates in the future because eventually we will hit $40 trillion (about $120,000 per person in the US) in debt and at just a 4% interest rate the annual interest due on the debt alone will be $1.4 trillion annually (about $4,300 per person in the US). It could surpass all other budget items. Defense could be cut by 50% but Social Security and Medicare will not because of a thing called Unfunded Liabilities. These are future liabilities at the current discounted net present value. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now, you will never hear a politician or the media discuss this number, whether they be Republican or Democrat because such a number, if the U.S. civilian population ever caught on, could start a revolution. Notice that we currently owe $452,104 for every man, woman and child in the country.', 'That unfunded liabilities number in 2019 was actually $122 trillion and has gone up $28.6 trillion in just 20 months. With additional federal government spending promised by the current Administration, it will continue to rise rapidly.', 'That is why it seems that the government is going bankrupt and has gone off the rails. With Revenue of just $3.5 trillion it will take 43 years to pay for just the unfunded liabilities ($150/$3.5) even if the government spent nothing on anything else. Of course, that is not possible so the probability that any of the debt will ever be repaid is close to zero.', 'The only way to solve this is to either default on the debt or to print so much money that the US Dollar will lose its reserve status which would undoubtedly result in a massive spike in inflation because the $US would weaken against all other major currencies making everything we import cost more. The Federal Reserve knows this and as more and more debt is issued, it continues to buy the debt to add to its balance sheet. This can only go on for so long before investors around the globe lose confidence in the $US. Then we will lose the reserve currency status as a better alternative is formulated. And there goes our advantage in global commerce and perhaps our ability to keep issuing debt without consequences.', 'Source: yardeni.com', 'Margin debt is at historically high levels and the stock market is riding the wave. This is a train wreck waiting to happen. When the market begins to fall, margin calls will require investors to liquidate stocks positions to meet margin requirements. That will lead to an additional drop in stock prices leading to more margin calls. You can see where this could go if it gets out of hand and at these nosebleed levels that is a recipe for disaster.', 'What does all this mean for the stock market? The best indicator to explain this comes from the greatest investor in history, Warren Buffett, and is called the Buffett Indicator.', 'Market Cap to GDP is a long-term valuation indicator that has become popular in recent years, thanks to Warren Buffett. Back in 2001, he remarked in a Fortune Magazine interview that "it is probably the best single measure of where valuations stand at any given moment." ', "So here is the master investor's prime indicator.", '', 'Source: AdvisorPerspectives.com', 'As you can see, in 2000 it was at 159.2% and in 2008 it was at 110%. Clearly, the stock market has gone off the rails again because that number is now at 209.1%. If the Federal Reserve were to ever start raising rates not only will the party be over in a millisecond, but the bubble pop would be crushing; that is why it is important to concentrate our investment strategy on "Capital Appreciation through Capital Preservation" by focusing on elite companies, with elite management and elite products. Because even though they may get hit as well when the big bang eventually does show up, each will recover quickly as they are the best of the best. ', '', 'Source: Accenture.com Investor Relations', "Besides the amazing growth rates in all categories, the one indicator that stands out is the New Bookings at the bottom right which is equal to a 39% increase over quarterly revenue. That tells us that business is booming, and that more success is coming. But this is Main Street talking on ACN's part and while Wall Street instead is concentrating on companies that issue new shares just to be able to pay the dividends to shareholders, Accenture instead is buying back its shares and retiring them.", 'Not only that, but because of its amazing free cash flow it has been able to scoop up 39 innovative companies that are doing innovative work just in the first 6 months of 2021. So, not only do we own a portfolio of amazing companies, but those same companies are buying up smaller elite companies and thus enhance each mothership in the progress.', 'In holding such killers in our portfolio, we can thus have a very conservative, as well as high growth, strategy in place that allows us to keep cash on hand, just in case the Federal Reserve messes up and pops the massive bubble it has created. That cash position is there for future ammo (dry powder) to be put to use in the same companies we own (and other elites that become bargains) but at reduced prices, due to any crash that happens. Thus, we are prepared for all scenarios that may show up and can sleep peacefully at night despite the madness that is all around us.', 'At Friedrich Global Research we are searching for what we believe will be the safest and best performing companies in which to buy stocks. We focus on free cash flow, efficient capital allocation, and consistently superior results to identify the highest quality management teams.  ', 'Interested? Go here to sign up today. ', '', 'This article was written by', 'Founder of Bern Factor LLC, an independent research and publishing firm located in Virginia. I have nearly 40 years of investing and analysis experience. I am a former CPA (1990 -2017) and became a CFA charter holder in 2000. I consider myself an expert in Quantitative and Qualitative analysis and have extensive experience in Technical Analysis. I also have a deep interest in stock market history and hold degrees in Economics (BS) and Management Information Systems (MBA). I have been actively involved with investment analysis since 1985 but have been a student of investing since the 1960s. I owned my first individual stock position while still in high school. I am a student of Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett. I have achieved a uniquely diverse experience from multiple careers that has allowed me to develop a broad perspective enabling me to look at the big picture of macroeconomics all the way down to the detail of a retail unit or factory floor. In my youth I was in retail, then served in reconnaissance during my tours in Vietnam. I have been a blue collar, union worker in a factory and a manager in services, hospitality and transportation as well as a manager of professional staffs. I have more than 20 years of experience each in both the public and private sectors. I have personal points of reference that many analysts will never have. I bring more to the table than just the theories and models I have studied or built. To understand more about my investing philosophy please visit my website.', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I am/we are long ACN.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. Nothing herein should be construed as an offer to buy or sell securities or to give individual investment advice.', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '']

Title: Keeping Your Portfolio Safe In An Unsafe Market 
 Content: ['The U.S. economy is riding on margin, just rolling along with asset prices (stocks, real estate, bonds, etc.) rising on a wave of debt. We may have higher to go, but the end could get messy.', 'The markets this past quarter have been on a bit of a roller coaster going up sharply in April and then dropping like a rock in May, only to recover in June. The U.S. economy could run into some problems in the not-too-distant future. What is an investor to do? Letâ\x80\x99s look at some of the problems first.', "The Federal Reserve's Balance sheet had grown from $1 trillion in 2008 to its current $8 trillion in 2021. If you do the math and annualize that rate, it comes out to a growth rate of 17.34%. Now the goal of the Federal Reserve has always been to keep its balance sheet under $1.5 Trillion except for emergencies like World War II and the Pandemic but going to $8 Trillion means they will have to taper by $6.5 Trillion at some point going forward unless it intends to monetize the debt. And if the Fed admits that it is monetizing debt it risks losing credibility. That could spell disaster for the US dollar. If you believe the Central Bank of the U.S. will ever be able to reduce its balance sheet by that much, then I have a bridge in Brooklyn that I can offer to sell to you real cheap. Why do I say that? Well, government spending has completely gone off the rails, that's why.", 'The Clock is Ticking on U.S. Debt. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'The national debt is at $28.4 Trillion right now but here is what it looks like when you chart it out and compare to where it was as far back as 1900:', '', 'Source: futuretimeline/Pinterest', 'As you can see 2020 was just $23.2 Trillion but recently we hit $28.4 Trillion for an annualized growth rate of 22.41% in just one year and in just one year we added $5.2 Trillion in debt. That is equal to the debt accumulated during all of the first 92 years on the chart above. And that could just be a start to what may be coming.', 'Up until 2000, Main Street has been able to keep up with the debt as our GDP (Gross Domestic Product) has come in around $22 Trillion, so you had about a 100% or so Debt to GDP ratio. But 2021 has changed all that and now we are at 128%. ', 'The Generation that survived World War II is considered the greatest generation and the comforts we have today are due to their defeating Hitler. The current Generation could unfortunately go down in history as the worst generation because of what is projected to happen by 2050 when we pass the baton to your grandchildren. Source: Brookings Institute', 'In 2020 our Debt to GDP was 100% and by 2050 the Congressional Budget Office projects it to be 200%, or twice what it was when we fought World War II. After the War, the greatest generation brought it down from 102% to 35% in 1980, but our generation can never do so, simply because the interest on the national debt will eventually be the largest annual federal expenditure someday. Currently the annual tax revenue comes in at: ', 'And here is where most of that money goes: ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now just those 4 budget items are equal to more than the revenue coming in, so anything else in the budget needs to be paid for by debt. Get ready for tax hikes folks!!! And even that will not be enough.', 'The interest on the debt is $400 billion annually (about $1,200 per person in the US) now but that is only because interest rates are so low. The Federal Reserve cannot raise interest rates in the future because eventually we will hit $40 trillion (about $120,000 per person in the US) in debt and at just a 4% interest rate the annual interest due on the debt alone will be $1.4 trillion annually (about $4,300 per person in the US). It could surpass all other budget items. Defense could be cut by 50% but Social Security and Medicare will not because of a thing called Unfunded Liabilities. These are future liabilities at the current discounted net present value. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now, you will never hear a politician or the media discuss this number, whether they be Republican or Democrat because such a number, if the U.S. civilian population ever caught on, could start a revolution. Notice that we currently owe $452,104 for every man, woman and child in the country.', 'That unfunded liabilities number in 2019 was actually $122 trillion and has gone up $28.6 trillion in just 20 months. With additional federal government spending promised by the current Administration, it will continue to rise rapidly.', 'That is why it seems that the government is going bankrupt and has gone off the rails. With Revenue of just $3.5 trillion it will take 43 years to pay for just the unfunded liabilities ($150/$3.5) even if the government spent nothing on anything else. Of course, that is not possible so the probability that any of the debt will ever be repaid is close to zero.', 'The only way to solve this is to either default on the debt or to print so much money that the US Dollar will lose its reserve status which would undoubtedly result in a massive spike in inflation because the $US would weaken against all other major currencies making everything we import cost more. The Federal Reserve knows this and as more and more debt is issued, it continues to buy the debt to add to its balance sheet. This can only go on for so long before investors around the globe lose confidence in the $US. Then we will lose the reserve currency status as a better alternative is formulated. And there goes our advantage in global commerce and perhaps our ability to keep issuing debt without consequences.', 'Source: yardeni.com', 'Margin debt is at historically high levels and the stock market is riding the wave. This is a train wreck waiting to happen. When the market begins to fall, margin calls will require investors to liquidate stocks positions to meet margin requirements. That will lead to an additional drop in stock prices leading to more margin calls. You can see where this could go if it gets out of hand and at these nosebleed levels that is a recipe for disaster.', 'What does all this mean for the stock market? The best indicator to explain this comes from the greatest investor in history, Warren Buffett, and is called the Buffett Indicator.', 'Market Cap to GDP is a long-term valuation indicator that has become popular in recent years, thanks to Warren Buffett. Back in 2001, he remarked in a Fortune Magazine interview that "it is probably the best single measure of where valuations stand at any given moment." ', "So here is the master investor's prime indicator.", '', 'Source: AdvisorPerspectives.com', 'As you can see, in 2000 it was at 159.2% and in 2008 it was at 110%. Clearly, the stock market has gone off the rails again because that number is now at 209.1%. If the Federal Reserve were to ever start raising rates not only will the party be over in a millisecond, but the bubble pop would be crushing; that is why it is important to concentrate our investment strategy on "Capital Appreciation through Capital Preservation" by focusing on elite companies, with elite management and elite products. Because even though they may get hit as well when the big bang eventually does show up, each will recover quickly as they are the best of the best. ', '', 'Source: Accenture.com Investor Relations', "Besides the amazing growth rates in all categories, the one indicator that stands out is the New Bookings at the bottom right which is equal to a 39% increase over quarterly revenue. That tells us that business is booming, and that more success is coming. But this is Main Street talking on ACN's part and while Wall Street instead is concentrating on companies that issue new shares just to be able to pay the dividends to shareholders, Accenture instead is buying back its shares and retiring them.", 'Not only that, but because of its amazing free cash flow it has been able to scoop up 39 innovative companies that are doing innovative work just in the first 6 months of 2021. So, not only do we own a portfolio of amazing companies, but those same companies are buying up smaller elite companies and thus enhance each mothership in the progress.', 'In holding such killers in our portfolio, we can thus have a very conservative, as well as high growth, strategy in place that allows us to keep cash on hand, just in case the Federal Reserve messes up and pops the massive bubble it has created. That cash position is there for future ammo (dry powder) to be put to use in the same companies we own (and other elites that become bargains) but at reduced prices, due to any crash that happens. Thus, we are prepared for all scenarios that may show up and can sleep peacefully at night despite the madness that is all around us.', 'At Friedrich Global Research we are searching for what we believe will be the safest and best performing companies in which to buy stocks. We focus on free cash flow, efficient capital allocation, and consistently superior results to identify the highest quality management teams.  ', 'Interested? Go here to sign up today. ', '', 'This article was written by', 'Founder of Bern Factor LLC, an independent research and publishing firm located in Virginia. I have nearly 40 years of investing and analysis experience. I am a former CPA (1990 -2017) and became a CFA charter holder in 2000. I consider myself an expert in Quantitative and Qualitative analysis and have extensive experience in Technical Analysis. I also have a deep interest in stock market history and hold degrees in Economics (BS) and Management Information Systems (MBA). I have been actively involved with investment analysis since 1985 but have been a student of investing since the 1960s. I owned my first individual stock position while still in high school. I am a student of Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett. I have achieved a uniquely diverse experience from multiple careers that has allowed me to develop a broad perspective enabling me to look at the big picture of macroeconomics all the way down to the detail of a retail unit or factory floor. In my youth I was in retail, then served in reconnaissance during my tours in Vietnam. I have been a blue collar, union worker in a factory and a manager in services, hospitality and transportation as well as a manager of professional staffs. I have more than 20 years of experience each in both the public and private sectors. I have personal points of reference that many analysts will never have. I bring more to the table than just the theories and models I have studied or built. To understand more about my investing philosophy please visit my website.', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I am/we are long ACN.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. DISCLAIMER: This analysis is not advice to buy or sell this or any stock; it is just pointing out an objective observation of unique patterns that developed from our research. Factual material is obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but the poster is not responsible for any errors or omissions, or for the results of actions taken based on information contained herein. Nothing herein should be construed as an offer to buy or sell securities or to give individual investment advice.', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '']

Title: Keeping Your Portfolio Safe In An Unsafe Market 
 Content: ['The U.S. economy is riding on margin, just rolling along with asset prices (stocks, real estate, bonds, etc.) rising on a wave of debt. We may have higher to go, but the end could get messy.', 'The markets this past quarter have been on a bit of a roller coaster going up sharply in April and then dropping like a rock in May, only to recover in June. The U.S. economy could run into some problems in the not-too-distant future. What is an investor to do? Letâ\x80\x99s look at some of the problems first.', "The Federal Reserve's Balance sheet had grown from $1 trillion in 2008 to its current $8 trillion in 2021. If you do the math and annualize that rate, it comes out to a growth rate of 17.34%. Now the goal of the Federal Reserve has always been to keep its balance sheet under $1.5 Trillion except for emergencies like World War II and the Pandemic but going to $8 Trillion means they will have to taper by $6.5 Trillion at some point going forward unless it intends to monetize the debt. And if the Fed admits that it is monetizing debt it risks losing credibility. That could spell disaster for the US dollar. If you believe the Central Bank of the U.S. will ever be able to reduce its balance sheet by that much, then I have a bridge in Brooklyn that I can offer to sell to you real cheap. Why do I say that? Well, government spending has completely gone off the rails, that's why.", 'The Clock is Ticking on U.S. Debt. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'The national debt is at $28.4 Trillion right now but here is what it looks like when you chart it out and compare to where it was as far back as 1900:', '', 'Source: futuretimeline/Pinterest', 'As you can see 2020 was just $23.2 Trillion but recently we hit $28.4 Trillion for an annualized growth rate of 22.41% in just one year and in just one year we added $5.2 Trillion in debt. That is equal to the debt accumulated during all of the first 92 years on the chart above. And that could just be a start to what may be coming.', 'Up until 2000, Main Street has been able to keep up with the debt as our GDP (Gross Domestic Product) has come in around $22 Trillion, so you had about a 100% or so Debt to GDP ratio. But 2021 has changed all that and now we are at 128%. ', 'The Generation that survived World War II is considered the greatest generation and the comforts we have today are due to their defeating Hitler. The current Generation could unfortunately go down in history as the worst generation because of what is projected to happen by 2050 when we pass the baton to your grandchildren. Source: Brookings Institute', 'In 2020 our Debt to GDP was 100% and by 2050 the Congressional Budget Office projects it to be 200%, or twice what it was when we fought World War II. After the War, the greatest generation brought it down from 102% to 35% in 1980, but our generation can never do so, simply because the interest on the national debt will eventually be the largest annual federal expenditure someday. Currently the annual tax revenue comes in at: ', 'And here is where most of that money goes: ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now just those 4 budget items are equal to more than the revenue coming in, so anything else in the budget needs to be paid for by debt. Get ready for tax hikes folks!!! And even that will not be enough.', 'The interest on the debt is $400 billion annually (about $1,200 per person in the US) now but that is only because interest rates are so low. The Federal Reserve cannot raise interest rates in the future because eventually we will hit $40 trillion (about $120,000 per person in the US) in debt and at just a 4% interest rate the annual interest due on the debt alone will be $1.4 trillion annually (about $4,300 per person in the US). It could surpass all other budget items. Defense could be cut by 50% but Social Security and Medicare will not because of a thing called Unfunded Liabilities. These are future liabilities at the current discounted net present value. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now, you will never hear a politician or the media discuss this number, whether they be Republican or Democrat because such a number, if the U.S. civilian population ever caught on, could start a revolution. Notice that we currently owe $452,104 for every man, woman and child in the country.', 'That unfunded liabilities number in 2019 was actually $122 trillion and has gone up $28.6 trillion in just 20 months. With additional federal government spending promised by the current Administration, it will continue to rise rapidly.', 'That is why it seems that the government is going bankrupt and has gone off the rails. With Revenue of just $3.5 trillion it will take 43 years to pay for just the unfunded liabilities ($150/$3.5) even if the government spent nothing on anything else. Of course, that is not possible so the probability that any of the debt will ever be repaid is close to zero.', 'The only way to solve this is to either default on the debt or to print so much money that the US Dollar will lose its reserve status which would undoubtedly result in a massive spike in inflation because the $US would weaken against all other major currencies making everything we import cost more. The Federal Reserve knows this and as more and more debt is issued, it continues to buy the debt to add to its balance sheet. This can only go on for so long before investors around the globe lose confidence in the $US. Then we will lose the reserve currency status as a better alternative is formulated. And there goes our advantage in global commerce and perhaps our ability to keep issuing debt without consequences.', 'Source: yardeni.com', 'Margin debt is at historically high levels and the stock market is riding the wave. This is a train wreck waiting to happen. When the market begins to fall, margin calls will require investors to liquidate stocks positions to meet margin requirements. That will lead to an additional drop in stock prices leading to more margin calls. You can see where this could go if it gets out of hand and at these nosebleed levels that is a recipe for disaster.', 'What does all this mean for the stock market? The best indicator to explain this comes from the greatest investor in history, Warren Buffett, and is called the Buffett Indicator.', 'Market Cap to GDP is a long-term valuation indicator that has become popular in recent years, thanks to Warren Buffett. Back in 2001, he remarked in a Fortune Magazine interview that "it is probably the best single measure of where valuations stand at any given moment." ', "So here is the master investor's prime indicator.", '', 'Source: AdvisorPerspectives.com', 'As you can see, in 2000 it was at 159.2% and in 2008 it was at 110%. Clearly, the stock market has gone off the rails again because that number is now at 209.1%. If the Federal Reserve were to ever start raising rates not only will the party be over in a millisecond, but the bubble pop would be crushing; that is why it is important to concentrate our investment strategy on "Capital Appreciation through Capital Preservation" by focusing on elite companies, with elite management and elite products. Because even though they may get hit as well when the big bang eventually does show up, each will recover quickly as they are the best of the best. ', '', 'Source: Accenture.com Investor Relations', "Besides the amazing growth rates in all categories, the one indicator that stands out is the New Bookings at the bottom right which is equal to a 39% increase over quarterly revenue. That tells us that business is booming, and that more success is coming. But this is Main Street talking on ACN's part and while Wall Street instead is concentrating on companies that issue new shares just to be able to pay the dividends to shareholders, Accenture instead is buying back its shares and retiring them.", 'Not only that, but because of its amazing free cash flow it has been able to scoop up 39 innovative companies that are doing innovative work just in the first 6 months of 2021. So, not only do we own a portfolio of amazing companies, but those same companies are buying up smaller elite companies and thus enhance each mothership in the progress.', 'In holding such killers in our portfolio, we can thus have a very conservative, as well as high growth, strategy in place that allows us to keep cash on hand, just in case the Federal Reserve messes up and pops the massive bubble it has created. That cash position is there for future ammo (dry powder) to be put to use in the same companies we own (and other elites that become bargains) but at reduced prices, due to any crash that happens. Thus, we are prepared for all scenarios that may show up and can sleep peacefully at night despite the madness that is all around us.', 'At Friedrich Global Research we are searching for what we believe will be the safest and best performing companies in which to buy stocks. We focus on free cash flow, efficient capital allocation, and consistently superior results to identify the highest quality management teams.  ', 'Interested? Go here to sign up today. ', '', 'This article was written by', 'Founder of Bern Factor LLC, an independent research and publishing firm located in Virginia. I have nearly 40 years of investing and analysis experience. I am a former CPA (1990 -2017) and became a CFA charter holder in 2000. I consider myself an expert in Quantitative and Qualitative analysis and have extensive experience in Technical Analysis. I also have a deep interest in stock market history and hold degrees in Economics (BS) and Management Information Systems (MBA). I have been actively involved with investment analysis since 1985 but have been a student of investing since the 1960s. I owned my first individual stock position while still in high school. I am a student of Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett. I have achieved a uniquely diverse experience from multiple careers that has allowed me to develop a broad perspective enabling me to look at the big picture of macroeconomics all the way down to the detail of a retail unit or factory floor. In my youth I was in retail, then served in reconnaissance during my tours in Vietnam. I have been a blue collar, union worker in a factory and a manager in services, hospitality and transportation as well as a manager of professional staffs. I have more than 20 years of experience each in both the public and private sectors. I have personal points of reference that many analysts will never have. I bring more to the table than just the theories and models I have studied or built. To understand more about my investing philosophy please visit my website.', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I am/we are long ACN.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. DISCLAIMER: This analysis is not advice to buy or sell this or any stock; it is just pointing out an objective observation of unique patterns that developed from our research. Factual material is obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but the poster is not responsible for any errors or omissions, or for the results of actions taken based on information contained herein. Nothing herein should be construed as an offer to buy or sell securities or to give individual investment advice.', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '']

Title: Keeping Your Portfolio Safe In An Unsafe Market 
 Content: ['The U.S. economy is riding on margin, just rolling along with asset prices (stocks, real estate, bonds, etc.) rising on a wave of debt. We may have higher to go, but the end could get messy.', 'The markets this past quarter have been on a bit of a roller coaster going up sharply in April and then dropping like a rock in May, only to recover in June. The U.S. economy could run into some problems in the not-too-distant future. What is an investor to do? Letâ\x80\x99s look at some of the problems first.', "The Federal Reserve's Balance sheet had grown from $1 trillion in 2008 to its current $8 trillion in 2021. If you do the math and annualize that rate, it comes out to a growth rate of 17.34%. Now the goal of the Federal Reserve has always been to keep its balance sheet under $1.5 Trillion except for emergencies like World War II and the Pandemic but going to $8 Trillion means they will have to taper by $6.5 Trillion at some point going forward unless it intends to monetize the debt. And if the Fed admits that it is monetizing debt it risks losing credibility. That could spell disaster for the US dollar. If you believe the Central Bank of the U.S. will ever be able to reduce its balance sheet by that much, then I have a bridge in Brooklyn that I can offer to sell to you real cheap. Why do I say that? Well, government spending has completely gone off the rails, that's why.", 'The Clock is Ticking on U.S. Debt. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'The national debt is at $28.4 Trillion right now but here is what it looks like when you chart it out and compare to where it was as far back as 1900:', '', 'Source: futuretimeline/Pinterest', 'As you can see 2020 was just $23.2 Trillion but recently we hit $28.4 Trillion for an annualized growth rate of 22.41% in just one year and in just one year we added $5.2 Trillion in debt. That is equal to the debt accumulated during all of the first 92 years on the chart above. And that could just be a start to what may be coming.', 'Up until 2000, Main Street has been able to keep up with the debt as our GDP (Gross Domestic Product) has come in around $22 Trillion, so you had about a 100% or so Debt to GDP ratio. But 2021 has changed all that and now we are at 128%. ', 'The Generation that survived World War II is considered the greatest generation and the comforts we have today are due to their defeating Hitler. The current Generation could unfortunately go down in history as the worst generation because of what is projected to happen by 2050 when we pass the baton to your grandchildren. Source: Brookings Institute', 'In 2020 our Debt to GDP was 100% and by 2050 the Congressional Budget Office projects it to be 200%, or twice what it was when we fought World War II. After the War, the greatest generation brought it down from 102% to 35% in 1980, but our generation can never do so, simply because the interest on the national debt will eventually be the largest annual federal expenditure someday. Currently the annual tax revenue comes in at: ', 'And here is where most of that money goes: ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now just those 4 budget items are equal to more than the revenue coming in, so anything else in the budget needs to be paid for by debt. Get ready for tax hikes folks!!! And even that will not be enough.', 'The interest on the debt is $400 billion annually (about $1,200 per person in the US) now but that is only because interest rates are so low. The Federal Reserve cannot raise interest rates in the future because eventually we will hit $40 trillion (about $120,000 per person in the US) in debt and at just a 4% interest rate the annual interest due on the debt alone will be $1.4 trillion annually (about $4,300 per person in the US). It could surpass all other budget items. Defense could be cut by 50% but Social Security and Medicare will not because of a thing called Unfunded Liabilities. These are future liabilities at the current discounted net present value. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now, you will never hear a politician or the media discuss this number, whether they be Republican or Democrat because such a number, if the U.S. civilian population ever caught on, could start a revolution. Notice that we currently owe $452,104 for every man, woman and child in the country.', 'That unfunded liabilities number in 2019 was actually $122 trillion and has gone up $28.6 trillion in just 20 months. With additional federal government spending promised by the current Administration, it will continue to rise rapidly.', 'That is why it seems that the government is going bankrupt and has gone off the rails. With Revenue of just $3.5 trillion it will take 43 years to pay for just the unfunded liabilities ($150/$3.5) even if the government spent nothing on anything else. Of course, that is not possible so the probability that any of the debt will ever be repaid is close to zero.', 'The only way to solve this is to either default on the debt or to print so much money that the US Dollar will lose its reserve status which would undoubtedly result in a massive spike in inflation because the $US would weaken against all other major currencies making everything we import cost more. The Federal Reserve knows this and as more and more debt is issued, it continues to buy the debt to add to its balance sheet. This can only go on for so long before investors around the globe lose confidence in the $US. Then we will lose the reserve currency status as a better alternative is formulated. And there goes our advantage in global commerce and perhaps our ability to keep issuing debt without consequences.', 'Source: yardeni.com', 'Margin debt is at historically high levels and the stock market is riding the wave. This is a train wreck waiting to happen. When the market begins to fall, margin calls will require investors to liquidate stocks positions to meet margin requirements. That will lead to an additional drop in stock prices leading to more margin calls. You can see where this could go if it gets out of hand and at these nosebleed levels that is a recipe for disaster.', 'What does all this mean for the stock market? The best indicator to explain this comes from the greatest investor in history, Warren Buffett, and is called the Buffett Indicator.', 'Market Cap to GDP is a long-term valuation indicator that has become popular in recent years, thanks to Warren Buffett. Back in 2001, he remarked in a Fortune Magazine interview that "it is probably the best single measure of where valuations stand at any given moment." ', "So here is the master investor's prime indicator.", '', 'Source: AdvisorPerspectives.com', 'As you can see, in 2000 it was at 159.2% and in 2008 it was at 110%. Clearly, the stock market has gone off the rails again because that number is now at 209.1%. If the Federal Reserve were to ever start raising rates not only will the party be over in a millisecond, but the bubble pop would be crushing; that is why it is important to concentrate our investment strategy on "Capital Appreciation through Capital Preservation" by focusing on elite companies, with elite management and elite products. Because even though they may get hit as well when the big bang eventually does show up, each will recover quickly as they are the best of the best. ', '', 'Source: Accenture.com Investor Relations', "Besides the amazing growth rates in all categories, the one indicator that stands out is the New Bookings at the bottom right which is equal to a 39% increase over quarterly revenue. That tells us that business is booming, and that more success is coming. But this is Main Street talking on ACN's part and while Wall Street instead is concentrating on companies that issue new shares just to be able to pay the dividends to shareholders, Accenture instead is buying back its shares and retiring them.", 'Not only that, but because of its amazing free cash flow it has been able to scoop up 39 innovative companies that are doing innovative work just in the first 6 months of 2021. So, not only do we own a portfolio of amazing companies, but those same companies are buying up smaller elite companies and thus enhance each mothership in the progress.', 'In holding such killers in our portfolio, we can thus have a very conservative, as well as high growth, strategy in place that allows us to keep cash on hand, just in case the Federal Reserve messes up and pops the massive bubble it has created. That cash position is there for future ammo (dry powder) to be put to use in the same companies we own (and other elites that become bargains) but at reduced prices, due to any crash that happens. Thus, we are prepared for all scenarios that may show up and can sleep peacefully at night despite the madness that is all around us.', 'At Friedrich Global Research we are searching for what we believe will be the safest and best performing companies in which to buy stocks. We focus on free cash flow, efficient capital allocation, and consistently superior results to identify the highest quality management teams.  ', 'Interested? Go here to sign up today. ', '', 'This article was written by', 'Founder of Bern Factor LLC, an independent research and publishing firm located in Virginia. I have nearly 40 years of investing and analysis experience. I am a former CPA (1990 -2017) and became a CFA charter holder in 2000. I consider myself an expert in Quantitative and Qualitative analysis and have extensive experience in Technical Analysis. I also have a deep interest in stock market history and hold degrees in Economics (BS) and Management Information Systems (MBA). I have been actively involved with investment analysis since 1985 but have been a student of investing since the 1960s. I owned my first individual stock position while still in high school. I am a student of Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett. I have achieved a uniquely diverse experience from multiple careers that has allowed me to develop a broad perspective enabling me to look at the big picture of macroeconomics all the way down to the detail of a retail unit or factory floor. In my youth I was in retail, then served in reconnaissance during my tours in Vietnam. I have been a blue collar, union worker in a factory and a manager in services, hospitality and transportation as well as a manager of professional staffs. I have more than 20 years of experience each in both the public and private sectors. I have personal points of reference that many analysts will never have. I bring more to the table than just the theories and models I have studied or built. To understand more about my investing philosophy please visit my website.', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I am/we are long ACN.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. DISCLAIMER: This analysis is not advice to buy or sell this or any stock; it is just pointing out an objective observation of unique patterns that developed from our research. Factual material is obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but the poster is not responsible for any errors or omissions, or for the results of actions taken based on information contained herein. Nothing herein should be construed as an offer to buy or sell securities or to give individual investment advice.', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Title: Keeping Your Portfolio Safe In An Unsafe Market 
 Content: ['The U.S. economy is riding on margin, just rolling along with asset prices (stocks, real estate, bonds, etc.) rising on a wave of debt. We may have higher to go, but the end could get messy.', 'The markets this past quarter have been on a bit of a roller coaster going up sharply in April and then dropping like a rock in May, only to recover in June. The U.S. economy could run into some problems in the not-too-distant future. What is an investor to do? Letâ\x80\x99s look at some of the problems first.', "The Federal Reserve's Balance sheet had grown from $1 trillion in 2008 to its current $8 trillion in 2021. If you do the math and annualize that rate, it comes out to a growth rate of 17.34%. Now the goal of the Federal Reserve has always been to keep its balance sheet under $1.5 Trillion except for emergencies like World War II and the Pandemic but going to $8 Trillion means they will have to taper by $6.5 Trillion at some point going forward unless it intends to monetize the debt. And if the Fed admits that it is monetizing debt it risks losing credibility. That could spell disaster for the US dollar. If you believe the Central Bank of the U.S. will ever be able to reduce its balance sheet by that much, then I have a bridge in Brooklyn that I can offer to sell to you real cheap. Why do I say that? Well, government spending has completely gone off the rails, that's why.", 'The Clock is Ticking on U.S. Debt. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'The national debt is at $28.4 Trillion right now but here is what it looks like when you chart it out and compare to where it was as far back as 1900:', '', 'Source: futuretimeline/Pinterest', 'As you can see 2020 was just $23.2 Trillion but recently we hit $28.4 Trillion for an annualized growth rate of 22.41% in just one year and in just one year we added $5.2 Trillion in debt. That is equal to the debt accumulated during all of the first 92 years on the chart above. And that could just be a start to what may be coming.', 'Up until 2000, Main Street has been able to keep up with the debt as our GDP (Gross Domestic Product) has come in around $22 Trillion, so you had about a 100% or so Debt to GDP ratio. But 2021 has changed all that and now we are at 128%. ', 'The Generation that survived World War II is considered the greatest generation and the comforts we have today are due to their defeating Hitler. The current Generation could unfortunately go down in history as the worst generation because of what is projected to happen by 2050 when we pass the baton to your grandchildren. Source: Brookings Institute', 'In 2020 our Debt to GDP was 100% and by 2050 the Congressional Budget Office projects it to be 200%, or twice what it was when we fought World War II. After the War, the greatest generation brought it down from 102% to 35% in 1980, but our generation can never do so, simply because the interest on the national debt will eventually be the largest annual federal expenditure someday. Currently the annual tax revenue comes in at: ', 'And here is where most of that money goes: ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now just those 4 budget items are equal to more than the revenue coming in, so anything else in the budget needs to be paid for by debt. Get ready for tax hikes folks!!! And even that will not be enough.', 'The interest on the debt is $400 billion annually (about $1,200 per person in the US) now but that is only because interest rates are so low. The Federal Reserve cannot raise interest rates in the future because eventually we will hit $40 trillion (about $120,000 per person in the US) in debt and at just a 4% interest rate the annual interest due on the debt alone will be $1.4 trillion annually (about $4,300 per person in the US). It could surpass all other budget items. Defense could be cut by 50% but Social Security and Medicare will not because of a thing called Unfunded Liabilities. These are future liabilities at the current discounted net present value. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now, you will never hear a politician or the media discuss this number, whether they be Republican or Democrat because such a number, if the U.S. civilian population ever caught on, could start a revolution. Notice that we currently owe $452,104 for every man, woman and child in the country.', 'That unfunded liabilities number in 2019 was actually $122 trillion and has gone up $28.6 trillion in just 20 months. With additional federal government spending promised by the current Administration, it will continue to rise rapidly.', 'That is why it seems that the government is going bankrupt and has gone off the rails. With Revenue of just $3.5 trillion it will take 43 years to pay for just the unfunded liabilities ($150/$3.5) even if the government spent nothing on anything else. Of course, that is not possible so the probability that any of the debt will ever be repaid is close to zero.', 'The only way to solve this is to either default on the debt or to print so much money that the US Dollar will lose its reserve status which would undoubtedly result in a massive spike in inflation because the $US would weaken against all other major currencies making everything we import cost more. The Federal Reserve knows this and as more and more debt is issued, it continues to buy the debt to add to its balance sheet. This can only go on for so long before investors around the globe lose confidence in the $US. Then we will lose the reserve currency status as a better alternative is formulated. And there goes our advantage in global commerce and perhaps our ability to keep issuing debt without consequences.', 'Source: yardeni.com', 'Margin debt is at historically high levels and the stock market is riding the wave. This is a train wreck waiting to happen. When the market begins to fall, margin calls will require investors to liquidate stocks positions to meet margin requirements. That will lead to an additional drop in stock prices leading to more margin calls. You can see where this could go if it gets out of hand and at these nosebleed levels that is a recipe for disaster.', 'What does all this mean for the stock market? The best indicator to explain this comes from the greatest investor in history, Warren Buffett, and is called the Buffett Indicator.', 'Market Cap to GDP is a long-term valuation indicator that has become popular in recent years, thanks to Warren Buffett. Back in 2001, he remarked in a Fortune Magazine interview that "it is probably the best single measure of where valuations stand at any given moment." ', "So here is the master investor's prime indicator.", '', 'Source: AdvisorPerspectives.com', 'As you can see, in 2000 it was at 159.2% and in 2008 it was at 110%. Clearly, the stock market has gone off the rails again because that number is now at 209.1%. If the Federal Reserve were to ever start raising rates not only will the party be over in a millisecond, but the bubble pop would be crushing; that is why it is important to concentrate our investment strategy on "Capital Appreciation through Capital Preservation" by focusing on elite companies, with elite management and elite products. Because even though they may get hit as well when the big bang eventually does show up, each will recover quickly as they are the best of the best. ', '', 'Source: Accenture.com Investor Relations', "Besides the amazing growth rates in all categories, the one indicator that stands out is the New Bookings at the bottom right which is equal to a 39% increase over quarterly revenue. That tells us that business is booming, and that more success is coming. But this is Main Street talking on ACN's part and while Wall Street instead is concentrating on companies that issue new shares just to be able to pay the dividends to shareholders, Accenture instead is buying back its shares and retiring them.", 'Not only that, but because of its amazing free cash flow it has been able to scoop up 39 innovative companies that are doing innovative work just in the first 6 months of 2021. So, not only do we own a portfolio of amazing companies, but those same companies are buying up smaller elite companies and thus enhance each mothership in the progress.', 'In holding such killers in our portfolio, we can thus have a very conservative, as well as high growth, strategy in place that allows us to keep cash on hand, just in case the Federal Reserve messes up and pops the massive bubble it has created. That cash position is there for future ammo (dry powder) to be put to use in the same companies we own (and other elites that become bargains) but at reduced prices, due to any crash that happens. Thus, we are prepared for all scenarios that may show up and can sleep peacefully at night despite the madness that is all around us.', 'At Friedrich Global Research we are searching for what we believe will be the safest and best performing companies in which to buy stocks. We focus on free cash flow, efficient capital allocation, and consistently superior results to identify the highest quality management teams.  ', 'Interested? Go here to sign up today. ', '', 'This article was written by', 'Founder of Bern Factor LLC, an independent research and publishing firm located in Virginia. I have nearly 40 years of investing and analysis experience. I am a former CPA (1990 -2017) and became a CFA charter holder in 2000. I consider myself an expert in Quantitative and Qualitative analysis and have extensive experience in Technical Analysis. I also have a deep interest in stock market history and hold degrees in Economics (BS) and Management Information Systems (MBA). I have been actively involved with investment analysis since 1985 but have been a student of investing since the 1960s. I owned my first individual stock position while still in high school. I am a student of Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett. I have achieved a uniquely diverse experience from multiple careers that has allowed me to develop a broad perspective enabling me to look at the big picture of macroeconomics all the way down to the detail of a retail unit or factory floor. In my youth I was in retail, then served in reconnaissance during my tours in Vietnam. I have been a blue collar, union worker in a factory and a manager in services, hospitality and transportation as well as a manager of professional staffs. I have more than 20 years of experience each in both the public and private sectors. I have personal points of reference that many analysts will never have. I bring more to the table than just the theories and models I have studied or built. To understand more about my investing philosophy please visit my website.', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I am/we are long ACN.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. Title: Keeping Your Portfolio Safe In An Unsafe Market 
 Content: ['The U.S. economy is riding on margin, just rolling along with asset prices (stocks, real estate, bonds, etc.) rising on a wave of debt. We may have higher to go, but the end could get messy.', 'The markets this past quarter have been on a bit of a roller coaster going up sharply in April and then dropping like a rock in May, only to recover in June. The U.S. economy could run into some problems in the not-too-distant future. What is an investor to do? Letâ\x80\x99s look at some of the problems first.', "The Federal Reserve's Balance sheet had grown from $1 trillion in 2008 to its current $8 trillion in 2021. If you do the math and annualize that rate, it comes out to a growth rate of 17.34%. Now the goal of the Federal Reserve has always been to keep its balance sheet under $1.5 Trillion except for emergencies like World War II and the Pandemic but going to $8 Trillion means they will have to taper by $6.5 Trillion at some point going forward unless it intends to monetize the debt. And if the Fed admits that it is monetizing debt it risks losing credibility. That could spell disaster for the US dollar. If you believe the Central Bank of the U.S. will ever be able to reduce its balance sheet by that much, then I have a bridge in Brooklyn that I can offer to sell to you real cheap. Why do I say that? Well, government spending has completely gone off the rails, that's why.", 'The Clock is Ticking on U.S. Debt. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'The national debt is at $28.4 Trillion right now but here is what it looks like when you chart it out and compare to where it was as far back as 1900:', '', 'Source: futuretimeline/Pinterest', 'As you can see 2020 was just $23.2 Trillion but recently we hit $28.4 Trillion for an annualized growth rate of 22.41% in just one year and in just one year we added $5.2 Trillion in debt. That is equal to the debt accumulated during all of the first 92 years on the chart above. And that could just be a start to what may be coming.', 'Up until 2000, Main Street has been able to keep up with the debt as our GDP (Gross Domestic Product) has come in around $22 Trillion, so you had about a 100% or so Debt to GDP ratio. But 2021 has changed all that and now we are at 128%. ', 'The Generation that survived World War II is considered the greatest generation and the comforts we have today are due to their defeating Hitler. The current Generation could unfortunately go down in history as the worst generation because of what is projected to happen by 2050 when we pass the baton to your grandchildren. Source: Brookings Institute', 'In 2020 our Debt to GDP was 100% and by 2050 the Congressional Budget Office projects it to be 200%, or twice what it was when we fought World War II. After the War, the greatest generation brought it down from 102% to 35% in 1980, but our generation can never do so, simply because the interest on the national debt will eventually be the largest annual federal expenditure someday. Currently the annual tax revenue comes in at: ', 'And here is where most of that money goes: ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now just those 4 budget items are equal to more than the revenue coming in, so anything else in the budget needs to be paid for by debt. Get ready for tax hikes folks!!! And even that will not be enough.', 'The interest on the debt is $400 billion annually (about $1,200 per person in the US) now but that is only because interest rates are so low. The Federal Reserve cannot raise interest rates in the future because eventually we will hit $40 trillion (about $120,000 per person in the US) in debt and at just a 4% interest rate the annual interest due on the debt alone will be $1.4 trillion annually (about $4,300 per person in the US). It could surpass all other budget items. Defense could be cut by 50% but Social Security and Medicare will not because of a thing called Unfunded Liabilities. These are future liabilities at the current discounted net present value. ', 'Source: usdebtclock.org', 'Now, you will never hear a politician or the media discuss this number, whether they be Republican or Democrat because such a number, if the U.S. civilian population ever caught on, could start a revolution. Notice that we currently owe $452,104 for every man, woman and child in the country.', 'That unfunded liabilities number in 2019 was actually $122 trillion and has gone up $28.6 trillion in just 20 months. With additional federal government spending promised by the current Administration, it will continue to rise rapidly.', 'That is why it seems that the government is going bankrupt and has gone off the rails. With Revenue of just $3.5 trillion it will take 43 years to pay for just the unfunded liabilities ($150/$3.5) even if the government spent nothing on anything else. Of course, that is not possible so the probability that any of the debt will ever be repaid is close to zero.', 'The only way to solve this is to either default on the debt or to print so much money that the US Dollar will lose its reserve status which would undoubtedly result in a massive spike in inflation because the $US would weaken against all other major currencies making everything we import cost more. The Federal Reserve knows this and as more and more debt is issued, it continues to buy the debt to add to its balance sheet. This can only go on for so long before investors around the globe lose confidence in the $US. Then we will lose the reserve currency status as a better alternative is formulated. And there goes our advantage in global commerce and perhaps our ability to keep issuing debt without consequences.', 'Source: yardeni.com', 'Margin debt is at historically high levels and the stock market is riding the wave. This is a train wreck waiting to happen. When the market begins to fall, margin calls will require investors to liquidate stocks positions to meet margin requirements. That will lead to an additional drop in stock prices leading to more margin calls. You can see where this could go if it gets out of hand and at these nosebleed levels that is a recipe for disaster.', 'What does all this mean for the stock market? The best indicator to explain this comes from the greatest investor in history, Warren Buffett, and is called the Buffett Indicator.', 'Market Cap to GDP is a long-term valuation indicator that has become popular in recent years, thanks to Warren Buffett. Back in 2001, he remarked in a Fortune Magazine interview that "it is probably the best single measure of where valuations stand at any given moment." ', "So here is the master investor's prime indicator.", '', 'Source: AdvisorPerspectives.com', 'As you can see, in 2000 it was at 159.2% and in 2008 it was at 110%. Clearly, the stock market has gone off the rails again because that number is now at 209.1%. If the Federal Reserve were to ever start raising rates not only will the party be over in a millisecond, but the bubble pop would be crushing; that is why it is important to concentrate our investment strategy on "Capital Appreciation through Capital Preservation" by focusing on elite companies, with elite management and elite products. Because even though they may get hit as well when the big bang eventually does show up, each will recover quickly as they are the best of the best. ', '', 'Source: Accenture.com Investor Relations', "Besides the amazing growth rates in all categories, the one indicator that stands out is the New Bookings at the bottom right which is equal to a 39% increase over quarterly revenue. That tells us that business is booming, and that more success is coming. But this is Main Street talking on ACN's part and while Wall Street instead is concentrating on companies that issue new shares just to be able to pay the dividends to shareholders, Accenture instead is buying back its shares and retiring them.", 'Not only that, but because of its amazing free cash flow it has been able to scoop up 39 innovative companies that are doing innovative work just in the first 6 months of 2021. So, not only do we own a portfolio of amazing companies, but those same companies are buying up smaller elite companies and thus enhance each mothership in the progress.', 'In holding such killers in our portfolio, we can thus have a very conservative, as well as high growth, strategy in place that allows us to keep cash on hand, just in case the Federal Reserve messes up and pops the massive bubble it has created. That cash position is there for future ammo (dry powder) to be put to use in the same companies we own (and other elites that become bargains) but at reduced prices, due to any crash that happens. Thus, we are prepared for all scenarios that may show up and can sleep peacefully at night despite the madness that is all around us.', 'At Friedrich Global Research we are searching for what we believe will be the safest and best performing companies in which to buy stocks. We focus on free cash flow, efficient capital allocation, and consistently superior results to identify the highest quality management teams.  ', 'Interested? Go here to sign up today. ', '', 'This article was written by', 'Founder of Bern Factor LLC, an independent research and publishing firm located in Virginia. I have nearly 40 years of investing and analysis experience. I am a former CPA (1990 -2017) and became a CFA charter holder in 2000. I consider myself an expert in Quantitative and Qualitative analysis and have extensive experience in Technical Analysis. I also have a deep interest in stock market history and hold degrees in Economics (BS) and Management Information Systems (MBA). I have been actively involved with investment analysis since 1985 but have been a student of investing since the 1960s. I owned my first individual stock position while still in high school. I am a student of Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett. I have achieved a uniquely diverse experience from multiple careers that has allowed me to develop a broad perspective enabling me to look at the big picture of macroeconomics all the way down to the detail of a retail unit or factory floor. In my youth I was in retail, then served in reconnaissance during my tours in Vietnam. I have been a blue collar, union worker in a factory and a manager in services, hospitality and transportation as well as a manager of professional staffs. I have more than 20 years of experience each in both the public and private sectors. I have personal points of reference that many analysts will never have. I bring more to the table than just the theories and models I have studied or built. To understand more about my investing philosophy please visit my website.', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I am/we are long ACN.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. Nothing herein should be construed as an offer to buy or sell securities or to give individual investment advice.', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
